intro.overview: This is it — your first live client engagement with real cloud infrastructure. Gulf Coast Healthcare Partners migrated their patient portal to Microsoft Azure after their ransomware incident 18 months ago. Jennifer Walsh (CISO) needs independent validation of network traffic patterns before her upcoming OCR audit. You’ll connect directly to the Azure-hosted portal, capture live traffic, and apply everything you learned in Weeks 1–2 to deliver a professional Engagement Packet.

mentorQuote.title: Senior Security Engineer (Blue Team Lead), NEXUS Security
mentorQuote.quote: Gulf Coast’s been paranoid since the ransomware hit. Jennifer’s team flagged some anomalous patterns but can’t determine if it’s malicious or just unusual clinical activity. Your job is to analyze the traffic and give her a confident answer. Every packet tells a story. This is what we’ve been training you for.

focusAreas: Cloud infrastructure interaction — SSH into Azure-hosted patient portal
Live Wireshark capture against real cloud infrastructure
All five Lab 3.2 analysis techniques applied to live traffic
Tiered discovery classification (Critical / High / Medium / Low)
Professional Engagement Packet — three-section structure (Timesheet, Runbook, Client Report)
Executive summary writing for a non-technical client audience
objectives: Connect to Azure-hosted Gulf Coast patient portal via SSH
Capture live network traffic using Wireshark on your host
Apply ALL analysis techniques from Lab 3.2 to live capture
Document findings with tiered discovery classification
Compile three weeks of work into professional Engagement Packet
Write executive summary addressing Jennifer Walsh’s concerns

callouts.title: Required: DOCX Format
callouts.text: Submit as .docx (Microsoft Word) only. PDF submissions will not be accepted. Use Word's built-in features for Table of Contents, page numbers, and citations.

callouts.title: Minimum Requirements
callouts.body: Your Engagement Packet must include: all required sections in order, 11 screenshots from this week (20–30) plus screenshots from Labs 3.1 and 3.2, VM hostname visible in every screenshot, and a minimum of 10 references. RFCs, man pages, and textbook chapters all count.

clientContext.description: Regional healthcare system (Houston, TX) with 3 hospitals, 15 clinics, and 2.1 million patient records. Experienced a ransomware attack 18 months ago; has since rebuilt their security program under CISO Jennifer Walsh. Hosting Provider: LoneStar DevOps (Austin, TX) — SaaS provider hosting Gulf Coast’s patient portal in Microsoft Azure; under security review as part of Gulf Coast’s vendor assessment program.

contacts.name: Jennifer Walsh
contacts.title: CISO, Gulf Coast Healthcare Partners
contacts.quote: We can’t secure what we can’t see. Your analysis helps us see what’s happening on our network.

youAlreadyHave.intro: If you followed directions in Labs 3.1 and 3.2, you already have most of the technical foundation for this Engagement Packet.

sections.title: From Week 7 — Lab 3.1: Network Addressing Fundamentals
From Week 8 — Lab 3.2: Protocol Analysis Tools
items: Protocol catalog (TCP, UDP, HTTP, HTTPS, DNS, ICMP)
IO Graph and Flow Graph examples

sections.title: New This Week — Lab 3.3

Live traffic analysis with tiered discoveries

callouts.title: You’re Already 60–70% Done
callouts.text: If you followed directions in Labs 3.1 and 3.2, you have all the technical foundation documented. This week is about applying those skills to a live engagement and wrapping it in professional packaging. Gather your Lab3-Screenshots folder and your notes — the hard work is already done.

timeGuide.timesheetNote: This is a client engagement — all time this week is billable to the client (Gulf Coast Healthcare Partners + LoneStar DevOps). This is significantly more than Foundation Labs because you’re delivering a complete professional report.

content.whatYoureDoing: Establish an SSH connection to the Azure-hosted Gulf Coast patient portal and capture the network traffic using Wireshark on your host machine.
content.whyItMatters: This is your first interaction with real cloud infrastructure. Unlike training PCAPs, this is live traffic to a production-like environment. The skills you practiced in Lab 3.2 now apply to actual client systems. Jennifer Walsh is counting on your analysis to determine if the anomalous traffic her team flagged represents a genuine threat.

steps.text: Open Wireshark on your host machine (not inside the Kali VM).
Identify your active network interface (Wi-Fi or Ethernet).
Resolve the server’s IP address from a terminal on your host:

whatThisDoes.concept: nslookup queries the Domain Name System (DNS) to translate a hostname into an IP address — the same process your browser performs behind the scenes every time you visit a website.
whatThisDoes.output: Look for a line showing Address: followed by an IP (e.g., 52.248.39.180). This is the IP you’ll use in your Wireshark capture filter.

steps.text:
Start a Wireshark capture on your active interface with a capture filter using the IP from nslookup:
host 52.248.39.180
(replace with the IP nslookup returned)

whatThisDoes.concept: Wireshark’s capture filters (BPF filters) resolve hostnames once at capture start and can silently fail. Using the IP directly is more reliable and is standard professional practice. You’ll still use the FQDN for SSH and browser connections — only the capture filter needs the IP.

steps.text: Open a terminal in your Kali VM.
SSH to the Gulf Coast portal:

whatThisDoes.concept: SSH (Secure Shell) creates an encrypted tunnel between your Kali VM and the remote server. All traffic within the tunnel is encrypted, which is why Wireshark will show SSH packets but cannot read their contents.
whatThisDoes.output: You’ll be asked to accept the host key fingerprint (type yes), then prompted for a password. After successful login, you’ll see a welcome banner identifying the Gulf Coast Healthcare Partners portal server.

flags.description: The username to authenticate as on the remote server
Separates the username from the hostname
The FQDN (Fully Qualified Domain Name) of the Azure-hosted server

steps.text: Password: NEXUSanalyst2026! (case-sensitive). Accept the host key fingerprint when prompted.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture your successful SSH connection showing the welcome banner and your Kali VM name visible in the prompt. Save as 20-azure-ssh-connection.png

content.part: Step 3 — Activity A: Browse via HTTPS

steps.text:
Open Firefox in your Kali VM and navigate to:
https://gulfcoast-portal.southcentralus.azurecontainer.io
Accept the certificate warning when prompted — this is a finding you will document.
Interact with the page (click links, explore).

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the Firefox certificate warning before you accept it. This self-signed certificate is a security finding. Save as 21-certificate-warning.png

content.part: Step 3 — Activity B: Browse via HTTP

steps.text:
In the same browser, navigate to the HTTP version:
http://gulfcoast-portal.southcentralus.azurecontainer.io
Notice anything different about how the site responds? Document your observation.

content.part: Step 3 — Activity C: Reconnaissance from Kali

steps.text: In your Kali VM (a separate terminal from your SSH session), request the HTTP headers:

whatThisDoes.concept: curl is a command-line tool for making HTTP requests. In security assessments, it’s preferred over a browser because it shows the raw server response without client-side rendering or caching. Examining response headers is one of the first steps in web application reconnaissance.
whatThisDoes.output: You’ll see headers like Server:, Content-Type:, and others. Pay close attention — what information is the server revealing about itself that an attacker could use?

flags.description: HEAD request — retrieves only the response headers, not the page content. Less noisy than a full GET request and reveals server configuration details.

steps.text: Now request the HTTPS headers for comparison:

whatThisDoes.concept: The same request but over HTTPS (encrypted). Comparing HTTP and HTTPS responses side-by-side can reveal configuration differences — servers sometimes expose different headers depending on the protocol.
whatThisDoes.output: Compare this response carefully to the HTTP version. Do you see any headers here that were not present in the HTTP response? Any differences are potential security findings.

flags.description: Insecure mode — tells curl to skip certificate verification. Required here because the server uses a self-signed certificate not trusted by default.
HEAD request — headers only, same as before.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture your terminal showing both curl outputs (HTTP and HTTPS headers) side by side or in sequence. Note the differences between the two responses. Save as 22-curl-header-comparison.png

content.part: Step 3 — Activity D: Server Exploration via SSH

steps.text: In your SSH session, list the web server’s document root:

whatThisDoes.concept: /var/www/ is the default web root on Linux servers. Listing its contents shows what web applications and directories the server hosts.
whatThisDoes.output: You should see one or more directories. Note the names — do any suggest older or legacy systems that might have weaker security controls?

steps.text: Check for Gulf Coast application files:

whatThisDoes.concept: /opt/ is where third-party and custom applications are typically installed. The wildcard (*) matches any file or directory starting with gulfcoast-.
whatThisDoes.output: Any files or directories here represent installed Gulf Coast applications. Explore their contents for configuration files, logs, or other artifacts.

steps.text: Examine the web server configuration:

whatThisDoes.concept: /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/ contains active site configurations for the nginx web server. Reading these files reveals how the server is configured: ports, SSL/TLS settings, served locations, and security headers (or lack thereof).
whatThisDoes.output: This is a goldmine for your assessment. Read carefully — look for SSL protocol versions, enabled locations, header settings, and misconfigurations. Many findings you’ll document in your report can be confirmed right here.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the nginx configuration output showing the server setup. Save as 23-nginx-server-config.png

steps.text: In Wireshark, go to Statistics → Protocol Hierarchy.
Verify you have at minimum: SSH (from terminal session), HTTP (from unencrypted browsing), TLS (from HTTPS browsing).
If you only see one protocol, complete the activities you missed before stopping.
Once verified, stop the capture and save as gulfcoast-live-capture.pcap.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the Protocol Hierarchy window (Statistics → Protocol Hierarchy) showing SSH, HTTP, and TLS traffic all present. Save as 24-protocol-hierarchy-verification.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: VPN blocking SSH: If you can’t connect, disable any VPN on your host machine first. Some campus networks also block outbound port 22 — try from a mobile hotspot.
Wrong interface: Wireshark runs on your HOST machine — all other activities (browsing, SSH, curl) happen inside your Kali VM
Firewall issues: Some campus networks block outbound SSH (port 22) — try from a mobile hotspot

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 18 covers cloud security fundamentals including virtual networks, shared responsibility models, and Azure-specific considerations.

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Successful SSH connection to Azure-hosted patient portal. PCAP file with SSH, HTTP, and TLS traffic (verified via Protocol Hierarchy). Screenshots #20–24 captured inline with each activity.

content.whatYoureDoing: A comprehensive analysis of the live traffic capture using all the Wireshark techniques you learned in Lab 3.2, with findings documented for Jennifer Walsh’s review.
content.whyItMatters: This is where your training pays off. Every technique you practiced with training PCAPs now applies to live client data. Jennifer needs to know: Is this traffic normal? Are there indicators of compromise? Should she escalate or stand down?

content.title: Use Your Runbook
content.text: You learned all five of these techniques in Lab 3.2. If you need a refresher on any workflow, open the runbook you built during that lab — that’s exactly what it’s for. A good runbook means you never have to relearn a tool from scratch.

steps.text: Apply ALL Lab 3.2 techniques to your live capture. Take a screenshot after each technique — these will form the evidence section of your Engagement Packet.

content.title: Technique 1: Protocol Distribution
content.text: Statistics → Protocol Hierarchy — shows the breakdown of all protocols with percentage breakdowns. If you captured #24 with enough detail, you may reuse that screenshot here. Save as 25-protocol-distribution.png

content.title: Technique 2: Endpoint Statistics
content.text: Statistics → Endpoints — shows every IP address and port that appeared in the capture. Look for unexpected endpoints. Save as 26-endpoint-statistics.png

content.title: Technique 3: TCP Stream Reconstruction
content.text: Right-click an HTTP packet → Follow → TCP Stream — reconstructs the full conversation. For HTTP traffic, you can read actual request and response in plain text. Save as 27-tcp-stream.png

content.title: Technique 4: IO Graph
content.text: Statistics → IO Graphs — visualizes traffic volume over time. Spikes can indicate automated scanning, data exfiltration, or denial-of-service activity. Save as 28-io-graph.png

content.title: Technique 5: Flow Graph
content.text: Statistics → Flow Graph — shows the sequence of packets as a ladder diagram. Useful for understanding TCP handshakes, TLS negotiations, and communication timing. Save as 29-flow-graph.png

content.title: Tiered Discovery Classification
content.subtitle: Classify each finding you document using this framework:
rows: Active compromise, data exfiltration, malware C2

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the most significant security finding you discovered during your analysis. Annotate what makes this a finding. Save as 30-notable-finding.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Wrong Wireshark interface: Wireshark must run on your HOST machine, not inside the Kali VM. All browsing, SSH, and curl commands happen inside Kali — Wireshark on the host captures all of it.

Single-protocol capture: Verify Protocol Hierarchy (Statistics → Protocol Hierarchy) before stopping. You need SSH, HTTP, and TLS all present. If you only see one protocol, complete the activities you skipped before stopping the capture.
Skipping server exploration: The nginx configuration and /var/www/ listing are findings, not optional steps. These outputs become evidence in your client report — document them carefully.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 18 covers cloud computing fundamentals including Azure's shared responsibility model, virtual networks, and cloud security considerations. Your SSH connection and live capture this week put Chapter 18 concepts into practice — cite it in your client report.

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Comprehensive analysis using all 5 Wireshark techniques. Tiered classification for each finding. Screenshots #25–30 captured inline. A clear answer to Jennifer’s question: escalate or stand down?

content.whatYoureDoing: Compile your three weeks of work into a professional Engagement Packet ready for delivery to Gulf Coast Healthcare Partners.
content.whyItMatters: Technical skills are only half the job. The ability to communicate findings clearly, professionally, and actionably is what separates good analysts from great ones. Jennifer Walsh doesn’t need raw data — she needs a report she can present to her board.

content.title: CISO, Gulf Coast Healthcare Partners
content.quote: We can’t secure what we can’t see. Your analysis helps us see what’s happening on our network. Give me clear findings I can present to my board — they don’t understand packet captures, but they understand business risk.

sections.label: Title Page
sections.description: Engagement title, client name, your name, date

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Technical jargon: Executive summary must be non-technical

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 9 covers network protocol fundamentals — the same protocols you cataloged in Lab 3.2 now appear in your live capture. Chapter 13 discusses IDS/IPS and anomaly detection; your protocol distribution analysis and endpoint statistics are exactly the techniques defenders use for network monitoring.

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Complete Engagement Packet with all sections. Executive summary answering Jennifer’s question. Minimum 10 references. Professional formatting ready for client delivery.

redTeam.label: What Red Team Learns
items: Your analysis revealed protocol distribution, active endpoints, and exposed services
An attacker conducting reconnaissance would use these same techniques to map the attack surface
The cleartext HTTP traffic and server header leakage you documented are exactly the footholds red teams exploit

blueTeam.label: What Blue Team Learns
items: Your protocol hierarchy analysis establishes baseline “normal” traffic
Anomaly detection requires knowing what’s expected — then anything outside that pattern becomes an alert candidate
The self-signed certificate and missing security headers you found are exactly what defenders audit for

purpleTeam.connection: The Gulf Coast scenario demonstrates purple team value: your analysis serves both offensive reconnaissance (what can an attacker learn?) and defensive assessment (what should we monitor?). This dual perspective is exactly what NEXUS delivers to Jennifer Walsh.

items.description: Successful SSH connection to Azure VM

canvasSubmission.text: Submit LastName_Lab3.3_EngagementPacket.docx to the Lab 3.3 assignment in Canvas before the deadline.

finalChecklist.finalWarning: PDF submissions receive an automatic zero. Submit .docx only.

penalties.condition: Per missing reference below minimum of 10

bonus.description: NetworkMiner Analysis: Install NetworkMiner on Windows and analyze the same PCAP. Compare and contrast its capabilities with Wireshark in an additional section.

gradingStandards.automaticZero: No submission, AI-generated content, fabricated screenshots, shared work, wrong format (PDF), or academic integrity violation.

needHelp.nextModule: Module 4: Threats & Attacks begins next week. You’ll shift focus to password security with Brazos Financial Group and Alamo Industries as clients.
needHelp.lateWorkPolicy: Late work is not accepted. Drop policies exist for emergencies.

permitted: Explain command syntax and tool functionality
prohibited: Complete hands-on exercises on your behalf
genAI.principle: AI is a tutor, not a ghostwriter.

whatsNext.text: Module 4: Threats & Attacks — Next module you'll shift focus to password security with Brazos Financial Group and Alamo Industries as clients. You'll use John the Ripper and Hashcat to crack Windows and Linux password hashes, then document findings and remediation recommendations for both clients.
